title: Koshinto metaphysics in Eva
wikitext:
[]: Koshinto metaphysics in Eva?             Quote Post by Noephon » Tue Jul 29, 2008 2:20 pm I was reading Motohisa Yamakage's book The Essence of Shinto earlier and there's a long chapter about the idea in certain schools of koshinto (='original Shinto', as opposed to State Shinto or modern schools) that the human spirit is in fact a manifestation of four different functions of the Great Original Spirit. This is called the shikon or Four Souls theory. The details of the theory are both abstruse and irrelevant, but one related idea is that the human body is composed of between 350 and 500 'sparks' which manifest out of the 'current of whirling spirits' that is the true reality of the universe. Collections of these sparks are held together by the Four Souls, which results in the creation of the naohinomitama, the individual soul, a bunrei or Child-Spirit of the Great Original Spirit. The naohinomitama both forms the individual personality and allows physical form to exist. It proceeds into the Inner Mountain, the realm of Kami, after death. Upon death, some of the Four Souls remain with the body, preventing it from disappearing entirely, but the absence of the naohinomitama causes it to slowly disintegrate. The existence of naohinomitama thus prevents us from uniting with the Great Original Spirit or with each other, but also allows interaction with others. So until such a time as a person no longer needs his or her naohinomitama and can fully unite with the Great Original Spirit, its continued existence is absolutely vital if we wish to maintain a sense of self and purpose above being mere eddies in the current of whirling spirits. Also, kamigami (and yes, this is the correct plural of the word 'kami') are viewed as having a hidden innermost part that never leaves the Inner Mountain and which forms the foundation of existence for those parts of the kami (shrine mirrors, sacred trees) which we can see. This is called kamizane , which literally translates to 'core of the kami.' In addition to being very confused, I thought that all this sounded familiar...

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Tue Jul 29, 2008 4:58 pm Sure all this came up before in another thread either here or on ANF, would be worth searching for.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Jul 30, 2008 1:43 pm I think it's more a coincidence than anything, since the Evaverse souls isn't made of four parts, from another reality, or destined to ascend to another plane. Nor do Angels have an uber "real" part hidden in the White Moon or something.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Jul 30, 2008 2:43 pm AuraTwilight wrote: I think it's more a coincidence than anything, since the Evaverse souls isn't made of four parts, from another reality, or destined to ascend to another plane. Nor do Angels have an uber "real" part hidden in the White Moon or something.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:01 am It's not like he has to copy it wholecloth to be clearly inspired by it.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:29 am AuraTwilight wrote: Sure, if you just plug in words. If you're deliberately looking for a connection, of course you're gonna find one. The brain is a pattern-making machine.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:37 am ANNO: I don't belong to any kind of organized religion, so I guess I could be considered agnostic. Japanese spiritualism holds that there is kami (spirit) in everything, and that's closer to my own beliefs.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu Jul 31, 2008 1:05 pm Is it really that unlikely that a Japanese man would apply a Shinto concept to the metaphysics of his Japanese anime made for his Japanese audience?

[]:             Quote Post by Noephon » Mon Aug 11, 2008 3:32 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Is it really that unlikely that a Japanese man would apply a Shinto concept to the metaphysics of his Japanese anime made for his Japanese audience?

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Aug 13, 2008 8:51 am Shinto related things would come up, no matter if intentional, or unintentional. Being Japanese means you will be unconciously affected, even if you are one of the Japanese catholics, or any other religion, by Shinto. I am currently drawing up everything there is in the other thread about this (plus buddhism)
